title: Metric MDS
text: Writing cost function respect distances makes pretty straightforward. However, need gradient respect parameters optimizing, coordinates city. Indicating (two-dimensional) vector represents coordinates city \(\) \(\mathbf{y_i}\), gradient cost function : \[\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y_i}} =   -2\sum_j \frac{r_{ij} - d_{ij}}{d_{ij}}   \left(    \mathbf{y_i - y_j}   \right) \] sum \(j\) cities. R code, : loop middle bit cryptic sweeps applys, job calculating \(\sum_j \mathbf{y_i} - \mathbf{y_j}\) part gradient row gradient matrix.
code: # R is the input distance matrix # D is the output distance matrix # y is a n x d matrix of output coordinates cost_grad <- function(R, D, y) {   K <- (R - D) / (D + 1.e-10)    G <- matrix(nrow = nrow(y), ncol = ncol(y))      for (i in 1:nrow(y)) {     dyij <- sweep(-y, 2, -y[i, ])     G[i, ] <- apply(dyij * K[, i], 2, sum)   }    as.vector(t(G)) * -2 }

code: mmds_fn <- function(par) {   R <- as.matrix(eurodist)   y <- matrix(par, ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)   D <- as.matrix(stats::dist(y))      cost_fun(R, D) } mmds_gr <- function(par) {   R <- as.matrix(eurodist)   y <- matrix(par, ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)   D <- as.matrix(stats::dist(y))    cost_grad(R, D, y) } set.seed(42) ed0 <- rnorm(attr(eurodist, 'Size') * 2)  res_euro <- mize(ed0, list(fn = mmds_fn, gr = mmds_gr),                  method = "L-BFGS", verbose = TRUE,                  grad_tol = 1e-5, check_conv_every = 10) #> 07:20:42 iter 0 f = 6.432e+08 g2 = 1.979e+05 nf = 1 ng = 1 step = 0 alpha = 0 #> 07:20:42 iter 10 f = 5.291e+07 g2 = 1.767e+04 nf = 26 ng = 26 step = 1291 alpha = 0.6507 #> 07:20:42 iter 20 f = 1.087e+07 g2 = 1.223e+04 nf = 39 ng = 39 step = 920.1 alpha = 0.4846 #> 07:20:42 iter 30 f = 3.357e+06 g2 = 129.3 nf = 49 ng = 49 step = 4.665 alpha = 0.5486 #> 07:20:42 iter 40 f = 3.356e+06 g2 = 0.2007 nf = 60 ng = 60 step = 0.03083 alpha = 1 #> 07:20:42 iter 50 f = 3.356e+06 g2 = 0.0003381 nf = 71 ng = 71 step = 2.863e-05 alpha = 1 plot_mmds <- function(coords, dist, ...) {   if (methods::is(coords, "numeric")) {     coords <- matrix(coords, ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)   }   graphics::plot(coords, type = 'n')   graphics::text(coords[, 1], coords[, 2], labels = labels(dist), ...) } plot_mmds(res_euro$par, eurodist, cex = 0.5) rot90 <- matrix(c(0, -1, 1, 0), ncol = 2) rotated <- t(rot90 %*% t(matrix(res_euro$par, ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE))) plot_mmds(rotated, eurodist, cex = 0.5)

code: #' Optimization by Classical Momentum #' #' @param par Starting point of vector of parameters to optimize. #' @param fn Objective function to optimize. Takes vector with length of #' \code{par} and returns a scalar. #' @param gr Gradient of the objective function \code{fn}. Takes vector with #' length of \code{par} and returns a vector of the same length. #' @param lr Learning rate. #' @param mu Momentum coefficient. #' @param max_iter Maximum number of iterations to optimize for. First iteration #' is always steepest descent. #' @return list with components: \code{par} final set of parameters; \code{f} #' value of \code{fn} evaluated at the returned set of parameters; \code{fs} #' vector of function evaluated after each iteration. cm <- function(par, fn, gr, lr, mu, max_iter = 10) {   fs <- rep(0, max_iter)    v <- rep(0, length(par))   for (i in 1:max_iter) {     g <- gr(par)     v <- mu * v - lr * g     par <- par + v      # store results     f <- fn(par)     fs[i] <- f   }    list(par = par, f = f, fs = fs) }

code: # Optimization by Nesterov Accelerated Gradient # # Return list also contains gd_fs: function evaluated after gradient descent # stage of each iteration; all: function evaluated after gradient descent # stage and momentum stage, in order. nag <- function(par, fn, gr, lr, mu, max_iter = 10) {   fs <- rep(0, max_iter)   gd_fs <- rep(0, max_iter)   all <- rep(0, max_iter * 2)    x_old <- rep(0, length(par))   for (i in 1:max_iter) {     # gradient descent stage     g <- gr(par)     x <- par - (lr * g)      # store gradient descent values     f <- fn(x)     gd_fs[i] <- f     all[i * 2 - 1] <- f      # momentum stage and update     par <- x + ifelse(i == 1, 0, mu) * (x - x_old)     x_old <- x      # store momentum values     f <- fn(par)     fs[i] <- f     all[i * 2] <- f   }    list(par = par, f = f, fs = fs, gd_fs = gd_fs, all = all) }

code: # Optimization by Sutskever Nesterov Momentum # # Return list also contains mu_fs: function evaluated after momentum # stage of each iteration; all: function evaluated after gradient descent # stage and momentum stage, in order. snag <- function(par, fn, gr, lr, mu, max_iter = 10) {   v <- rep(0, length(par))    fs <- rep(0, max_iter)   mu_fs <- rep(0, max_iter)   all <- rep(0, max_iter * 2)    for (i in 1:max_iter) {     # momentum stage and update parameters     mu_step <- mu * v     par <- par + mu_step      # store momentum results     f <- fn(par)     mu_fs[i] <- f     all[i * 2 - 1] <- f      # gradient descent stage     g <- gr(par)     gd_step <- -lr * g      # update and store velocity for next step     par <- par + gd_step     v <- mu_step + gd_step      # store gradient descent results     f <- fn(par)     fs[i] <- f     all[i * 2] <- f   }    list(par = par, f = f, fs = fs, mu_fs = mu_fs, all = all) }

code: # Optimization by Bengio Nesterov Momentum bnag <- function(par, fn, gr, lr, mu, max_iter = 10) {   fs <- rep(0, max_iter)    v <- rep(0, length(par))   for (i in 1:max_iter) {     g <- gr(par)     # here we approximate mu_{t+1} * mu_{t} as mu^2     # works because mu is constant across all time steps in these examples     par <- par + mu * mu * v - (1 + mu) * lr * g     v <- mu * v - lr * g      # store results     f <- fn(par)     fs[i] <- f   }    list(par = par, f = f, fs = fs) }

code: # Optimization by Dozat's expression for Nesterov momentum dnag <- function(par, fn, gr, lr, mu, max_iter = 10) {   fs <- rep(0, max_iter)    # mprime, the recursive variable   mpt <- rep(0, length(par))   mumu <- mu * mu    v <- rep(0, length(par))   for (i in 1:max_iter) {     g <- gr(par)      s <- lr * -g     v <- (1 + mu) * s + mumu * mpt     par <- par + v      # update mprime for next step     mpt <- mu * mpt + s     # store results     f <- fn(par)     fs[i] <- f   }    list(par = par, f = f, fs = fs) }

code: # Optimization by Nesterov Accelerated Gradient, using a momentum-style # update expression. mnag <- function(par, fn, gr, lr, mu, max_iter = 10) {   fs <- rep(0, max_iter)    v <- rep(0, length(par))   for (i in 1:max_iter) {     g <- gr(par)     v <- mu * (v - lr * g) - lr * g     par <- par + v      # setup v for the next iteration by removing the old gradient contribution     v <- v + lr * g      # store results     f <- fn(par)     fs[i] <- f   }    list(par = par, f = f, fs = fs) }

code: # Sutskever Nesterov Momentum consistent with NAG # # Extra parameter wait: wait this number of extra iterations before applying # momentum. Needed only to sync up with other implementations of nesterov # momentum: set wait to 1 to make mu_f at iter i match the output of the # other implementations at iter i-1. snagc <- function(par, fn, gr, lr, mu, max_iter = 10, wait = 0) {   v <- rep(0, length(par))    fs <- rep(0, max_iter)   mu_fs <- rep(0, max_iter)   all <- rep(0, max_iter * 2)    for (i in 1:max_iter) {     # momentum stage and update parameters     mu_step <- ifelse(i > wait + 1, mu, 0) * v     par <- par + mu_step      # store momentum results     f <- fn(par)     mu_fs[i] <- f     all[i * 2 - 1] <- f      # gradient descent stage     g <- gr(par)     gd_step <- -lr * g      # update and store velocity for next step     par <- par + gd_step     v <- mu_step + gd_step      # store gradient descent results     f <- fn(par)     fs[i] <- f     all[i * 2] <- f   }    list(par = par, f = f, fs = fs, mu_fs = mu_fs, all = all) }

title: Stateful Optimization
text: can see, greater power mize_step control iteration, comes greater responsibility. also need decide stop iterating. Apart par opt, components returned result list might help: f - function value, calculated par. methods don’t , can course generate via rb_fg$fn(par). g - gradient vector, calculated par. ’s present, obviously ’s nothing stop calculating rb_fg$gr(par) . nf - number function evaluations carried far (.e. since initialization). opt also keeping track , coordinates mize_step, don’t need manually update steps. ng - number gradient evaluations carried far. treat optimizer, opt, black box examine horrific innards, except check whether opt$error non-NULL. ’s anything NULL, means something really bad happened optimization, likely NaN Inf calculated gradient. can happen poorly chosen starting point, combination descent method line search doesn’t guarantee descent, aggressive momentum scheme likely adaptive learning rate technique like delta-bar-delta. Monitoring function value size change par iterations can help spot imminent divergence. Taking account, ’s self-contained example, removes now un-necessary batching, minor error checking, keeps track best parameters seen far (although combination optimizer problem, don’t worry ):
code: # Create the optimizer opt <- make_mize(method = "BFGS")  # Pretend we don't have access to the function or starting point until later rb_fg <- list(    fn = function(x) { 100 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) ^ 2 + (1 - x[1]) ^ 2  },    gr = function(x) { c( -400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),                           200 *        (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])) }) rb0 <- c(-1.2, 1)  # Initialize opt <- mize_init(opt = opt, par = rb0, fg = rb_fg)  # Store the best seen parameters in case something goes wrong par <- rb0 par_best <- par f_best <- rb_fg$fn(par_best)  for (i in 1:30) {   mize_res <- mize_step(opt = opt, par = par, fg = rb_fg)   par <- mize_res$par   opt <- mize_res$opt      # Do whatever you want with the data at each iteration      if (opt$is_terminated) {     # Something bad happened     break   }   if (mize_res$f < f_best) {     f_best <- mize_res$f     par_best <- par   } }  # optimized result is in par_best par_best #> [1] 0.9294066 0.8642370 f_best #> [1] 0.005002828

title: Stateful Optimization
text: return value mize_step provides function gradient values. like access information, mize_step_summary function can extract conveniently: mize_step_summary takes opt, par fg like mize_step , also optionally wants par_old argument. value par previous iteration, calculates size step taken iteration. Information available return value mize_step_summary includes: iter iteration number. f function value, ’s available, set convergence tolerance requires calculation (see ). g2n gradient l2 (Euclidean) norm, grad_tol non-NULL (see Convergence section ). ginfn gradient infinity norm, ginf_tol non-NULL (also see Convergence section ). nf number function evaluations far course optimization. ng number gradient evaluations far course optimization. step step size iteration. alpha size line search value found gradient descent stage. won’t step even optimizers don’t use extra momentum stage total step size normally value alpha multiplied magnitude gradient. mu momentum stage used, momentum coefficient. opt optimizer updated function gradient counts, f, g2n, ginfn calculated. many cases, f, g2n ginfn require recalculation (aren’t calculated), safe side, always reassign opt return value mize_step_summary. ’s modified version previous example, log information mize_step_summary. ’re going go 10 iterations avoid much output.
code: # Create optimizer and do one step of optimization as usual opt <- make_mize(method = "BFGS", par = rb0, fg = rb_fg) par <- rb0 mize_res <- mize_step(opt = opt, par = par, fg = rb_fg) step_info <- mize_step_summary(mize_res$opt, mize_res$par, rb_fg, par_old = par)  # info that's already available in mize_res step_info$f #> [1] 19.49933 step_info$ng #> [1] 3 step_info$nf #> [1] 3 # and some extra step_info$step #> [1] 0.02168573 step_info$alpha #> [1] 9.31247e-05 # Create the optimizer opt <- make_mize(method = "BFGS", par = rb0, fg = rb_fg)  par <- rb0 for (i in 1:10) {   par_old <- par   mize_res <- mize_step(opt = opt, par = par, fg = rb_fg)   par <- mize_res$par   opt <- mize_res$opt    # step info   step_info <- mize_step_summary(opt, par, rb_fg, par_old)   opt <- step_info$opt   message(paste(     Map(function(x) { paste0(x, " = ", formatC(step_info[[x]])) },          c("iter", "f", "nf", "ng", "step")),      collapse = ", ")) } #> iter = 1, f = 19.5, nf = 3, ng = 3, step = 0.02169 #> iter = 2, f = 11.57, nf = 4, ng = 4, step = 0.04729 #> iter = 3, f = 4.281, nf = 5, ng = 5, step = 0.09808 #> iter = 4, f = 4.144, nf = 6, ng = 6, step = 0.01427 #> iter = 5, f = 4.14, nf = 7, ng = 7, step = 0.002249 #> iter = 6, f = 4.136, nf = 8, ng = 8, step = 0.002942 #> iter = 7, f = 4.128, nf = 9, ng = 9, step = 0.00557 #> iter = 8, f = 4.114, nf = 10, ng = 10, step = 0.01061 #> iter = 9, f = 4.086, nf = 11, ng = 11, step = 0.02048 #> iter = 10, f = 2.604, nf = 14, ng = 14, step = 0.8382

title: Stateful Optimization
text: example now manually looped 30 iterations stopped. sophisticated stopping criteria available. Three changes needed: initializing optimizer, passing par fg either make_mize mize_init, also pass termination criteria: end loop, calling mize_step_summary, pass return value function mize_check_convergence. returns updated version opt indicate optimization stop setting opt$is_terminated boolean flag: Note don’t need manually assign opt value comes mize_step_summary, check_mize_convergence handles . Instead manually looping loop can use (!opt$is_terminated). opt$is_terminated TRUE, can find caused optimization looking opt$terminate$. using opt$is_terminated now, set TRUE meant something awful occurred, like infinity NaN gradient. check_mize_convergence also uses flag, now expanded meaning just indicates optimization cease, necessarily catastrophe occurred. ’s still worth checking opt$is_terminated set mize_step anything rest loop assumes gradient function value finite (e.g. comparing real number boolean condition). Apart just maximum number iterations, variety options relate convergence. separate vignette covers convergence options, parameters mentioned can passed make_mize mize_init. Whatever options use, setting max_iter good idea avoid infinite loop. ’s example repeated , time using check_mize_convergence control number iterations, rather loop:
code: opt <- make_mize(method = "BFGS", par = rb0, fg = rb_fg, max_iter = 30) # or opt <- make_mize(method = "BFGS") opt <- mize_init(opt = opt, par = rb0, fg = rb_fg, max_iter = 30) step_info <- mize_step_summary(opt, par, rb_fg, par_old) opt <- check_mize_convergence(step_info) # Create the optimizer opt <- make_mize(method = "BFGS")  rb_fg <- list(    fn = function(x) { 100 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) ^ 2 + (1 - x[1]) ^ 2  },    gr = function(x) { c( -400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),                           200 *        (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])) }) rb0 <- c(-1.2, 1)  # Initialize and set convergence criteria opt <- mize_init(opt = opt, par = rb0, fg = rb_fg, max_iter = 30)  # Store the best seen parameters in case something goes wrong par <- rb0 par_best <- par f_best <- rb_fg$fn(par_best)  while (!opt$is_terminated) {   mize_res <- mize_step(opt = opt, par = par, fg = rb_fg)   par <- mize_res$par   opt <- mize_res$opt      # Do whatever you want with the data at each iteration      if (opt$is_terminated) {     # Something bad happened     break   }   if (mize_res$f < f_best) {     f_best <- mize_res$f     par_best <- par   }      step_info <- mize_step_summary(opt, par, rb_fg, par_old)   # Do something with the step info if you'd like   # Check convergence   opt <- check_mize_convergence(step_info) }  # optimized result is in par_best par_best #> [1] 1 1 f_best #> [1] 0

title: mize
text: Unconstrained numerical optimization algorithms implemented R. mize can used standalone optimizer like stats::optim(), can integrated packages creating stateful optimizer handling iterations, convergence, logging externally. mize includes Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS), limited-memory BFGS (L-BFGS), conjugate gradient (CG), Nesterov accelerated gradient (NAG), momentum-based methods, related line searches.

code: library(mize)  rosenbrock_fg <- list(   fn = function(x) {     100 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])^2 + (1 - x[1])^2   },   gr = function(x) {     c(       -400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),       200 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])     )   } )  res <- mize(c(-1.2, 1), rosenbrock_fg, method = "L-BFGS") res$par res$f opt <- make_mize(method = "L-BFGS", par = c(-1.2, 1), fg = rosenbrock_fg) par <- c(-1.2, 1)  for (i in seq_len(30)) {   step <- mize_step(opt, par, rosenbrock_fg)   par <- step$par   opt <- step$opt }

title: Create an Optimizer — make_mize
text: method Optimization method. See 'Details' mize(). norm_direction TRUE, steepest descent direction normalized unit length. Useful adaptive step size methods previous step size used initialize next iteration. scale_hess TRUE, approximation inverse Hessian scaled according method described Nocedal Wright (approximating eigenvalue). Applies methods BFGS (scaling applied first step) L-BFGS (scaling applied every iteration). Ignored otherwise. memory number updates store using L-BFGS method. Ignored otherwise. Must positive integer. cg_update Type update use "CG" method. details see "CG" subsection "Optimization Methods" section. Ignored method "CG". preconditioner Type preconditioner use Truncated Newton. Leave blank set  "L-BFGS" use limited memory BFGS preconditioner. Use "memory" parameter control number updates store. Applies method = "TN", "CG", ignored otherwise. tn_init Type initialization use inner loop Truncated Newton. Use 0 use zero vector (usual TN initialization), "previous" use final result previous iteration, suggested Martens (2010). Applies method = "TN", ignored otherwise. tn_exit Type exit criterion use terminating inner CG loop Truncated Newton method. title: Create an Optimizer — make_mize
code: # Function to optimize and starting point rosenbrock_fg <- list(   fn = function(x) {     100 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])^2 + (1 - x[1])^2   },   gr = function(x) {     c(       -400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),       200 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])     )   } ) rb0 <- c(-1.2, 1)  # Create an optimizer and initialize it for use with the Rosenbrock function opt <- make_mize(method = "L-BFGS", par = rb0, fg = rosenbrock_fg)  # Create optimizer without initialization opt <- make_mize(method = "L-BFGS")  # Need to call mize_init separately: opt <- mize_init(opt, rb0, rosenbrock_fg)

title: Initialize the Optimizer. — mize_init
code: # Create an optimizer opt <- make_mize(method = "L-BFGS")  # Function to optimize and starting point defined after creating optimizer rosenbrock_fg <- list(   fn = function(x) {     100 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])^2 + (1 - x[1])^2   },   gr = function(x) {     c(       -400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),       200 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])     )   } ) rb0 <- c(-1.2, 1)  # Initialize with function and starting point before commencing optimization opt <- mize_init(opt, rb0, rosenbrock_fg)  # Finally, can commence the optimization loop par <- rb0 for (iter in 1:3) {   res <- mize_step(opt, par, rosenbrock_fg)   par <- res$par   opt <- res$opt }

title: One Step of Optimization — mize_step
code: rosenbrock_fg <- list(   fn = function(x) {     100 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])^2 + (1 - x[1])^2   },   gr = function(x) {     c(       -400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),       200 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])     )   } ) rb0 <- c(-1.2, 1)  opt <- make_mize(   method = "SD", line_search = "const", step0 = 0.0001,   par = rb0, fg = rosenbrock_fg ) par <- rb0 for (iter in 1:3) {   res <- mize_step(opt, par, rosenbrock_fg)   par <- res$par   opt <- res$opt }

title: Mize Step Summary — mize_step_summary
code: rb_fg <- list(   fn = function(x) {     100 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])^2 + (1 - x[1])^2   },   gr = function(x) {     c(       -400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1]) - 2 * (1 - x[1]),       200 * (x[2] - x[1] * x[1])     )   } ) rb0 <- c(-1.2, 1)  opt <- make_mize(method = "BFGS", par = rb0, fg = rb_fg, max_iter = 30) mize_res <- mize_step(opt = opt, par = rb0, fg = rb_fg) # Get info about first step, use rb0 to compare new par with initial value step_info <- mize_step_summary(mize_res$opt, mize_res$par, rb_fg, rb0)

title: mize (development version)
text: Fixed several optimizer edge cases found cleanup. New function: check_mize_convergence(), compare analytic gradient finite-different approximation. mize() now returns additive status fields status fields: converged, status, message, plus explicit best/last result fields (best_par, best_f, last_par, last_f).

title: mize 0.2.0
text: New method: Truncated Newton (method = "TN"). Can controlled using tn_init tn_exit options. New method: SR1 (method = "SR1"), falling back BFGS direction descent direction found. New option preconditioner, applies conjugate gradient truncated newton methods. value currently available preconditioner = "L-BFGS" uses L-BFGS estimate inverse Hessian preconditioning. number updates store preconditioner controlled memory parameter, just using method = "L-BFGS". BFGS, SR1, L-BFGS methods now make use user-supplied inverse Hessian function provided. input fg list, supply function hi, takes par vector input. function can return matrix (obviously great idea memory use), vector, latter assumed diagonal matrix. ls_max_alpha (line_search = "-Thuente" ): sets maximum value alpha can attained line search. ls_max_alpha_mult (Wolfe-type line search ): sets maximum value can attained ratio initial guess alpha current line search, final value alpha previous line search. Used stop line searches diverging due large initial guesses. ls_safe_cubic (line_search = "-Thuente" ): TRUE, use safe-guarded cubic modification suggested Xie Schlick. cg_update = "prfr", “PR-FR” (Polak-Ribiere/Fletcher-Reeves) conjugate gradient update suggested Gilbert Nocedal.
